Indications: Schizophrenia in adults and adolescents ≥ 13 years
Bipolar I disorder (acute manic or mixed episodes) in adults and pediatric patients ≥ 10 years
Adjunctive treatment of major depressive disorder (MDD) in adults
Irritability associated with autistic disorder (ages 6–17 years)
Tourette's disorder (ages 6–18 years)
PACriteria: ICD-10 diagnosis consistent with indication (e.g., F20.x schizophrenia, F31.x bipolar I disorder, F33.x major depressive disorder)
Documented moderate-to-severe symptom burden (e.g., PANSS score elevation, YMRS ≥ 20, or PHQ-9 ≥ 10 for MDD)
Failure of ≥ 6 weeks of first-line therapy (e.g., SSRI/SNRI for MDD, mood stabilizer for bipolar disorder)
For schizophrenia: inadequate response or intolerance to ≥ 1 antipsychotic
For adjunct MDD: inadequate response to ≥ 1 antidepressant at therapeutic dose
Baseline metabolic panel and weight documented
Assessment for extrapyramidal symptoms (EPS) risk
No active untreated psychosis-related substance intoxication

FirstLine: Bipolar disorder: mood stabilizer (e.g., lithium or valproate)
MDD: SSRI or SNRI for ≥ 6 weeks at therapeutic dose
SecondLine: Add or switch to aripiprazole after inadequate response
Typical starting dose 10–15 mg/day (schizophrenia), 2–5 mg/day (adjunct MDD)

Medicaid: Requires documented failure of first-line pharmacotherapy
Quantity limits based on FDA dosing
MedicarePartD: Step therapy with generic antidepressants or mood stabilizers
Formulary tier may vary
CommercialPlans: Prior authorization for adjunctive MDD indication common
Documentation of symptom scale scores often required

ReauthorizationRequirements: Improvement in symptom severity (e.g., ≥ 20% reduction in PANSS or ≥ 5-point reduction in PHQ-9 within 6–12 weeks)
Stabilization of mood episodes (YMRS reduction ≥ 50% for bipolar mania)
No significant metabolic adverse effects (e.g., weight gain > 7% baseline)
Continued psychiatric follow-up documentation

2.Title: APA Practice Guideline for the Treatment of Patients with Schizophrenia

ABILIFY_V2.Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient has a confirmed psychiatric diagnosis (e.g., F20.x schizophrenia or F33.x major depressive disorder) with persistent moderate-to-severe symptoms despite ≥ 6 weeks of first-line pharmacotherapy at therapeutic dosing. Objective symptom scales (e.g., PANSS, PHQ-9 ≥ 10) demonstrate clinically significant impairment. Per APA practice guidelines, escalation to or augmentation with an atypical antipsychotic such as aripiprazole is appropriate after inadequate response to initial therapy. Reauthorization will require ≥ 20% symptom reduction within 6–12 weeks and monitoring for metabolic effects. Delaying appropriate pharmacologic escalation increases risk of relapse, hospitalization, suicidality, and functional decline. Abilify is medically necessary and consistent with FDA-approved indications and guideline-directed care.

BENLYSTA_V2.Class: B-lymphocyte stimulator (BLyS) inhibitor (monoclonal antibody; IV or SC)

Indications: Active systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in patients ≥ 5 years receiving standard therapy
Active lupus nephritis in patients ≥ 5 years receiving standard therapy
PACriteria: ICD-10 SLE diagnosis (M32.x)
Fulfillment of 2019 ACR/EULAR classification criteria
Positive ANA and/or anti-dsDNA antibodies
SLEDAI ≥ 6 or documented moderate-to-severe disease activity
Evidence of active serologic disease (elevated anti-dsDNA and/or low C3/C4)
Persistent disease activity despite hydroxychloroquine unless contraindicated
Inability to taper prednisone to ≤ 7.5 mg/day without flare
Prior trial of conventional immunosuppressant (e.g., MMF 2–3 g/day ≥ 12 weeks or AZA 2–3 mg/kg/day ≥ 12 weeks)
For lupus nephritis: UPCR ≥ 1 g/g or biopsy-proven Class III/IV/V
Partial or inadequate renal response after 6–12 months of induction therapy

SLEStandardTherapy: Glucocorticoid taper attempt to ≤ 5–7.5 mg/day within 6 months
LupusNephritisProtocol: Escalate if partial renal response not achieved by 6–12 months

Medicaid: Requires failure of ≥ 1 immunosuppressant
Documentation of steroid dependence
MedicarePartD: Objective SLEDAI documentation required
Reauthorization based on ≥ 4-point SLEDAI reduction
CommercialPlans: Trial of MMF or AZA typically required
Autoantibody-positive restriction common

ReauthorizationRequirements: ≥ 4-point reduction in SLEDAI within 6 months
Reduction in flare frequency
Steroid taper achieved to ≤ 7.5 mg/day
For lupus nephritis: ≥ 30–50% reduction in proteinuria with stable eGFR

2.Title: 2025 American College of Rheumatology Guideline for the Treatment of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus

3.Title: 2019 Update of the EULAR Recommendations for the Management of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus

BENLYSTA_V2.Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient has confirmed systemic lupus erythematosus (ICD-10 M32.x) with persistent moderate-to-severe disease activity (SLEDAI ≥ 6), active serologies (elevated anti-dsDNA and/or low complement), and steroid dependence despite optimized hydroxychloroquine and ≥ 12 weeks of conventional immunosuppressive therapy. Per the 2025 American College of Rheumatology guideline and updated EULAR recommendations, belimumab is appropriate in patients with inadequate response to standard therapy. For lupus nephritis, escalation is supported when partial renal response is not achieved after 6–12 months of induction. Reauthorization will require ≥ 4-point SLEDAI improvement and objective reduction in proteinuria where applicable. Delaying biologic escalation increases risk of organ damage, flare frequency, and glucocorticoid-related morbidity. Belimumab is medically necessary and consistent with FDA-approved indications and contemporary rheumatology guidelines.

CPAP_V2.Class: Durable Medical Equipment (DME) – Positive Airway Pressure Therapy

Indications: Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) with apnea-hypopnea index (AHI) ≥ 15 events/hour
OSA with AHI 5–14 events/hour with symptoms (e.g., excessive daytime sleepiness) or comorbidities (e.g., hypertension, CAD, stroke)
PACriteria: ICD-10 diagnosis of obstructive sleep apnea (G47.33)
Documented polysomnography or home sleep study
AHI ≥ 15 events/hour OR AHI 5–14 with qualifying symptoms/comorbidities
Baseline Epworth Sleepiness Scale documented when applicable
Face-to-face clinical evaluation prior to testing
No central sleep apnea predominance unless using adaptive PAP device
Documentation of CPAP titration study or auto-PAP prescription parameters

InitialEvaluation: Comprehensive sleep evaluation and documentation of symptoms
Polysomnography or home sleep study confirming OSA
TreatmentInitiation: Typical pressure range 5–20 cm H₂O depending on titration results

Medicaid: Requires sleep study documentation
AHI thresholds must meet medical necessity criteria
MedicarePartD: Initial 12-week trial period
Compliance defined as ≥ 4 hours/night on ≥ 70% of nights
CommercialPlans: Similar AHI and compliance documentation required
DME supplier network restrictions may apply

ReauthorizationRequirements: Objective usage ≥ 4 hours/night on ≥ 70% of nights during initial 90 days
Documentation of symptom improvement (e.g., reduced daytime sleepiness)
Follow-up visit within 31–90 days documenting benefit

1.Title: Medicare National Coverage Determination for CPAP Therapy

2.Title: American Academy of Sleep Medicine Clinical Practice Guideline for OSA

CPAP_V2.Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient has documented obstructive sleep apnea (ICD-10 G47.33) confirmed by sleep study demonstrating an apnea-hypopnea index (AHI) ≥ 15 events/hour, meeting criteria for CPAP therapy. The patient also reports excessive daytime sleepiness with documented Epworth Sleepiness Scale elevation. Per Medicare National Coverage Determination and American Academy of Sleep Medicine guidelines, CPAP is medically necessary for OSA meeting AHI thresholds. Reauthorization requires objective adherence defined as ≥ 4 hours/night on ≥ 70% of nights within the first 90 days and documented symptomatic improvement. Untreated OSA increases risk of cardiovascular disease, stroke, and metabolic complications. CPAP therapy is medically necessary and consistent with national coverage criteria.

Indications: Prolonged mechanical ventilation
Failure to wean after ≥21 days ventilation
Chronic critical illness
Multisystem organ dysfunction
Complex tracheostomy management
High-acuity respiratory care
PACriteria: RESPIRATORY FAILURE / VENTILATOR DEPENDENCE:
Mechanical ventilation ≥21 consecutive days OR ≥2 failed spontaneous breathing trials
FiO2 requirement ≥40% OR PEEP ≥5 cm H2O within prior 72 hours
Respiratory therapy requirement ≥2 sessions per day
Tracheostomy management with ongoing ventilatory support
MULTISYSTEM MEDICAL COMPLEXITY:
At least 2 active organ system dysfunctions (e.g., respiratory + renal)
Hemodynamic instability within prior 72 hours requiring hospital-level monitoring
Frequent laboratory monitoring ≥3 times per week
FAILURE OF LOWER LEVEL OF CARE:
Documented evaluation and failure of Skilled Nursing Facility or IRF placement due to ongoing ventilator dependence and medical instability
Patient not stable for SNF-level nursing ratios or intermittent physician oversight
PHYSICIAN INTENSITY AND HOSPITAL-LEVEL REQUIREMENT:
Daily physician evaluation required
Need for coordinated multidisciplinary respiratory and medical management consistent with hospital-level care
TRAJECTORY AND LENGTH-OF-STAY JUSTIFICATION:
Anticipated length of stay >25 days based on ventilator weaning trajectory
Structured ventilator liberation plan requiring gradual reduction in ventilator hours

PayerType: Medicare LTCH Prospective Payment System qualification criteria
Medicare Advantage plans applying CMS LTCH standards

ReauthorizationRequirements: Documented reduction in ventilator hours per day over 14-day intervals
Serial improvement in oxygenation parameters
Objective reassessment of organ dysfunction markers
Ongoing documentation of need for daily physician oversight
Reevaluation for transition to lower level of care when stability achieved

1.Title: Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services. Long-Term Care Hospital Prospective Payment System (LTCH PPS)

2.Title: American Thoracic Society Statement on Chronic Critical Illness

DANIEL_LTAC_V4_20260331.Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: The patient meets criteria for Long-Term Acute Care Hospital admission due to prolonged mechanical ventilation exceeding 21 consecutive days and failure of multiple spontaneous breathing trials. Current respiratory status requires FiO2 ≥40% and structured ventilator weaning with respiratory therapy at least twice daily. The patient additionally demonstrates multisystem organ dysfunction requiring frequent laboratory monitoring and daily physician-directed management. Skilled Nursing Facility and IRF placement were formally evaluated and deemed inappropriate due to persistent ventilator dependence and medical instability, representing documented failure of lower level of care. Hospital-level monitoring remains medically necessary given ongoing respiratory failure, hemodynamic vulnerability within the prior 72 hours, and need for coordinated multidisciplinary critical care recovery. CMS LTCH qualification standards recognize prolonged mechanical ventilation as a defining criterion for LTAC-level care, and specialty society guidance supports structured ventilator weaning in chronic critical illness populations. Given the escalation timeline exceeding 21 days, objective oxygenation thresholds, and continued instability, transfer to or continuation in LTAC is medically necessary. Transition to a lower level of care will be reconsidered once ventilator liberation milestones and organ stability benchmarks are achieved.

CertaintyScale.High: We are confident the true effect lies close to the estimate.
CertaintyScale.Moderate: The true effect is likely close but may differ substantially.
CertaintyScale.Low: The true effect may be substantially different.
CertaintyScale.VeryLow: The true effect is very uncertain.

PrimaryOutcomeDomains.Domain: Ventilator Weaning Success in Chronic Critical Illness
PrimaryOutcomeDomains.Rationale: Prolonged mechanical ventilation ≥21 days benefits from structured weaning protocols in LTAC settings when lower-acuity facilities cannot provide daily physician-directed ventilator management.
SupportingReferences: American Thoracic Society Chronic Critical Illness Statement

DEXCOM_V2.Generic: Continuous Glucose Monitoring System (e.g., Dexcom G6/G7)
DEXCOM_V2.Class: Continuous Glucose Monitoring Device (Durable Medical Equipment)

Indications: Continuous glucose monitoring in patients with diabetes mellitus requiring intensive insulin therapy
Adjunct to blood glucose monitoring for improved glycemic control
PACriteria: ICD-10 diagnosis of Type 1 diabetes (E10.x) or Type 2 diabetes (E11.x)
Use of multiple daily insulin injections (≥ 3 per day) or insulin pump therapy
Documented self-monitoring of blood glucose ≥ 4 times/day prior to CGM initiation (if required by payer)
Recent A1C ≥ 7.0% OR documented history of hypoglycemia (< 70 mg/dL)
Documented episodes of hypoglycemia unawareness or nocturnal hypoglycemia
Provider evaluation and prescription for CGM
Patient/caregiver training documented

BaselineManagement: Optimized insulin regimen (basal-bolus or pump therapy)
Structured glucose monitoring with fingerstick SMBG
TechnologyEscalation: Initiate CGM when glycemic targets not achieved or hypoglycemia risk present
Sensor replacement every 10 days (G6) or per device specifications

Medicaid: Requires documentation of insulin regimen
A1C or hypoglycemia criteria often required
MedicarePartD: Requires insulin-treated diabetes
Face-to-face visit within 6 months prior to ordering
Continued use contingent on documented adherence
CommercialPlans: Prior authorization required
Hypoglycemia documentation or uncontrolled A1C often required

ReauthorizationRequirements: Continued insulin therapy
Demonstrated adherence to CGM use (≥ 70% active sensor time)
Improvement in glycemic metrics (e.g., increased Time in Range ≥ 10%)
Reduction in hypoglycemia episodes (< 70 mg/dL)

1.Title: FDA Device Labeling – Dexcom CGM Systems

2.Title: American Diabetes Association Standards of Care in Diabetes – 2024

DEXCOM_V2.Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient has insulin-treated diabetes mellitus (ICD-10 E10.x/E11.x) with suboptimal glycemic control (A1C ≥ 7.0%) and documented hypoglycemia (< 70 mg/dL). Despite optimized basal-bolus therapy and structured self-monitoring, glycemic variability persists. Continuous glucose monitoring is recommended by the American Diabetes Association for patients on intensive insulin regimens to improve Time in Range and reduce hypoglycemia. Reauthorization will require ≥ 70% sensor adherence and documented improvement in glycemic metrics including ≥ 10% increase in Time in Range. Delaying CGM therapy increases risk of severe hypoglycemia and long-term microvascular complications. Dexcom CGM is medically necessary and consistent with FDA authorization and contemporary diabetes standards.

Indications: Stroke prevention in nonvalvular atrial fibrillation (NVAF)
Treatment of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism (PE)
Reduction in risk of recurrent DVT and PE
Prophylaxis of DVT following hip or knee replacement surgery
PACriteria: ICD-10: I48.x (NVAF), I82.x (DVT), I26.x (PE), Z96.641/Z96.651 (hip/knee replacement)
For NVAF: CHA2DS2-VASc score ≥ 2 (men) or ≥ 3 (women)
No active pathological bleeding
Baseline renal function (CrCl) ≥ 15 mL/min documented within 3 months
Not indicated for mechanical heart valves or moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis
Dose reduction criteria met if ≥ 2 of the following: age ≥ 80 years, body weight ≤ 60 kg, serum creatinine ≥ 1.5 mg/dL
No concomitant strong dual CYP3A4 and P-gp inhibitors unless dose adjusted
No history of hypersensitivity to apixaban

NVAF: If CHA2DS2-VASc ≥ 2 (men) or ≥ 3 (women) and no contraindication, initiate apixaban
Reduce to 2.5 mg twice daily if ≥ 2 dose-reduction criteria met
DVT_PE_Treatment: Initiate 10 mg twice daily for 7 days
Reassess bleeding risk and adherence at 3 months
Extended_VTE_Prevention: After ≥ 6 months of therapy, reduce to 2.5 mg twice daily for secondary prevention
PostOp_Prophylaxis: Initiate 2.5 mg twice daily 12–24 hours after surgery once hemostasis achieved

Medicaid: Requires diagnosis confirmation and renal function documentation
May require documentation of warfarin intolerance or clinical rationale for DOAC
MedicarePartD: Covered for FDA indications with renal documentation
Tier placement may require prior authorization
CommercialPlans: Often preferred DOAC for NVAF and VTE indications
Requires documentation of CHA2DS2-VASc score and bleeding risk assessment

ReauthorizationRequirements: For NVAF: continued CHA2DS2-VASc indication and no major bleeding at 6–12 months
For VTE: reassessment of recurrence risk at 3–6 months
Renal function monitoring every 6–12 months
Documented adherence to prescribed twice-daily regimen

2.Title: CHEST Guideline and Expert Panel Report: Antithrombotic Therapy for VTE Disease (2021)

3.Title: AHA/ACC/HRS Guideline for the Management of Patients With Atrial Fibrillation

ELIQUIS_V3.Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient meets FDA-approved criteria for apixaban therapy. For NVAF, the CHA2DS2-VASc score is ≥ 2 (men) or ≥ 3 (women), indicating anticoagulation to reduce stroke risk. Apixaban is guideline-preferred over warfarin due to lower intracranial hemorrhage risk and predictable pharmacokinetics. For DVT/PE, apixaban provides evidence-based oral therapy with defined dosing transition (10 mg twice daily for 7 days followed by 5 mg twice daily). Delaying anticoagulation increases risk of thromboembolism and mortality. Apixaban is medically necessary and consistent with FDA labeling and contemporary guidelines.

Indications: Type 2 diabetes mellitus in adults and pediatric patients ≥10 years for glycemic control as adjunct to diet and exercise
To reduce risk of hospitalization for heart failure in adults with T2DM and established cardiovascular disease or multiple CV risk factors
Heart failure (HFrEF or HFpEF) to reduce cardiovascular death and heart failure hospitalization
Chronic kidney disease (eGFR ≥25 mL/min/1.73 m²) to reduce sustained eGFR decline, ESKD, CV death, and HF hospitalization
PACriteria: ICD-10: E11.x (T2DM), I50.x (HF), or N18.x (CKD)
For T2DM: baseline A1C ≥7.0% despite ≥3 months of metformin unless contraindicated
For HF: documented NYHA class II–IV with LVEF ≤40% (HFrEF) OR LVEF >40% (HFpEF) with elevated natriuretic peptides
For CKD: eGFR 25–75 mL/min/1.73 m² with UACR ≥200 mg/g despite ACEi/ARB therapy
Not for type 1 diabetes due to DKA risk
No history of serious hypersensitivity to dapagliflozin
Renal function assessed prior to initiation
Volume status assessed and corrected before initiation

Type2Diabetes: Lifestyle modification + metformin titrated to maximally tolerated dose
Add SGLT2 inhibitor when A1C remains ≥7.0% after ≥3 months
HeartFailure: Guideline-directed medical therapy (ACEi/ARB/ARNI + beta blocker + MRA as indicated)
Add dapagliflozin 10 mg once daily regardless of diabetes status
ChronicKidneyDisease: ACE inhibitor or ARB at maximally tolerated dose
Add dapagliflozin 10 mg daily if eGFR ≥25 mL/min/1.73 m²

Medicaid: Requires diagnosis-specific documentation (A1C, EF %, or eGFR/UACR)
Metformin trial required for diabetes unless contraindicated
MedicarePartD: Heart failure and CKD indications generally covered with documentation
May require confirmation of guideline-directed therapy
CommercialPlans: Prior authorization common for diabetes indication
Often preferred for HF or CKD due to guideline endorsement

ReauthorizationRequirements: For diabetes: ≥0.5–1.0% A1C reduction within 3–6 months
For HF: reduced HF hospitalization or stable NYHA class after 6 months
For CKD: stabilization or slower decline in eGFR at 6–12 months
Continued adherence and absence of DKA or severe adverse effects

2.Title: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes – 2024

3.Title: KDIGO 2022 Clinical Practice Guideline for Diabetes Management in CKD

4.Title: 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure

FARXIGA_V2.Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient meets criteria for dapagliflozin therapy under FDA-approved indications. In Type 2 diabetes mellitus, the patient demonstrates A1C ≥7.0% despite optimized metformin therapy. Per ADA Standards of Care 2024, SGLT2 inhibitors are recommended for patients with T2DM and cardiovascular or renal risk. In heart failure (HFrEF or HFpEF), dapagliflozin 10 mg daily reduces cardiovascular death and HF hospitalization independent of diabetes status per AHA/ACC/HFSA guidelines. In CKD with eGFR ≥25 mL/min/1.73 m² and albuminuria ≥200 mg/g, KDIGO 2022 recommends SGLT2 inhibitors to reduce progression to ESKD and CV events. Delaying initiation increases risk of heart failure hospitalization, progressive renal decline, and cardiovascular mortality. Farxiga is medically necessary and consistent with FDA labeling and contemporary multidisciplinary guidelines.

Indications: Moderate-to-severe rheumatoid arthritis in adults
Moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis
Psoriatic arthritis
Ankylosing spondylitis
Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis
Juvenile idiopathic arthritis
PACriteria: Confirmed diagnosis per ICD-10 code (e.g., M05.x for RA, L40.x for psoriasis, K50.x for Crohn's)
Moderate-to-severe disease activity (e.g., DAS28 > 3.2 for RA or BSA ≥ 10% for psoriasis)
Failure of ≥ 3 months of first-line therapy (e.g., methotrexate ≥ 15 mg/week for RA)
Inadequate response or intolerance to ≥ 1 conventional DMARD
For IBD: inadequate response to corticosteroids or immunomodulators
Baseline TB screening negative
Hepatitis B screening documented
No active serious infection

SecondLine: Initiate TNF inhibitor after inadequate response
Humira dosing: 40 mg subcutaneously every other week (RA standard)

Medicaid: Requires failure of conventional DMARD
TB screening documentation required
MedicarePartD: Step therapy with methotrexate common
Specialty pharmacy benefit
CommercialPlans: Biosimilar step therapy often required
Prior authorization with disease activity score documentation

Reduction in BSA involvement for psoriasis ≥ 50%
For IBD: documented clinical remission or reduced flare frequency
No serious infection or adverse events

2.Title: American College of Rheumatology Guideline for the Treatment of Rheumatoid Arthritis

HUMIRA_V2.Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient has a confirmed inflammatory autoimmune condition with moderate-to-severe disease activity despite ≥ 12 weeks of first-line therapy (e.g., methotrexate ≥ 15 mg/week for rheumatoid arthritis). Disease activity remains elevated (e.g., DAS28 > 3.2), and the patient meets criteria for escalation to biologic therapy per American College of Rheumatology guidelines. Baseline TB and hepatitis screening have been completed and are negative. Humira is FDA-approved for this indication and medically necessary given inadequate response to conventional therapy. Reauthorization will require objective improvement including ≥ 1.2 reduction in DAS28 or ≥ 50% improvement in BSA involvement for psoriasis within 3–6 months. Delaying biologic escalation increases risk of joint damage, disability, and systemic complications.

Indications: Adjunct to diet and exercise to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus
PACriteria: ICD-10 diagnosis of Type 2 diabetes mellitus (E11.x)
Baseline A1C ≥ 7.0% despite lifestyle modification
Trial of metformin at maximally tolerated dose for ≥ 3 months unless contraindicated
If ASCVD, CKD, or HF present, documentation of comorbidity
Inadequate glycemic control after ≥ 3 months of oral therapy
No personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2
Baseline A1C documented within past 3 months
Baseline weight documented

FirstLine: Metformin titrated to maximally tolerated dose
SecondLine: Addition of GLP-1 RA, dual incretin agonist, or SGLT2 inhibitor based on comorbidity profile
Initiate Mounjaro 2.5 mg weekly for 4 weeks, increase to 5 mg weekly; may titrate in 2.5 mg increments every 4 weeks to maximum 15 mg weekly

Medicaid: Requires metformin trial unless contraindicated
A1C ≥ 7.0% documentation required
MedicarePartD: Step therapy with metformin
Quantity limits per 28-day supply
CommercialPlans: Prior authorization required
May require trial of alternative GLP-1 receptor agonist

ReauthorizationRequirements: A1C reduction ≥ 0.5–1.0% within 3–6 months
Continued use contingent on adherence
Weight reduction documentation if applicable

2.Title: American Diabetes Association Standards of Care in Diabetes – 2024

MOUNJARO_V2.Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient has Type 2 diabetes mellitus (ICD-10 E11.x) with persistent hyperglycemia (A1C ≥ 7.0%) despite optimized metformin therapy and lifestyle modification for ≥ 3 months. Per ADA Standards of Care, escalation to a GLP-1 receptor agonist or dual incretin therapy is recommended when glycemic targets are not achieved. Mounjaro (tirzepatide) is FDA-approved for glycemic control in adults with T2DM and offers significant A1C reduction and weight benefit. Reauthorization will require ≥ 0.5–1.0% A1C reduction within 3–6 months. Delaying escalation increases risk of microvascular and macrovascular complications. Mounjaro is medically necessary and consistent with FDA-approved indications and evidence-based guidelines.

Indications: Adjunct to diet and exercise to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus¹
Reduction of major adverse cardiovascular events in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus and established cardiovascular disease¹
PACriteria: ICD-10 diagnosis of type 2 diabetes mellitus (E11.x)
Age ≥18 years
Baseline A1C ≥7.0% unless contraindication to alternative agents
Documented trial of metformin at maximally tolerated dose for ≥3 months unless contraindicated
Inadequate glycemic control despite oral therapy (A1C above individualized target)
For cardiovascular indication: documented ASCVD (e.g., prior MI, stroke, PAD)
BMI ≥25 kg/m² (if weight reduction also targeted)
Failure, intolerance, or contraindication to at least one additional antihyperglycemic agent if required by payer

FirstLine: Metformin titrated to maximally tolerated dose for ≥3 months
SecondLine: Addition of GLP-1 receptor agonist or SGLT2 inhibitor based on comorbidities
Consider insulin if A1C ≥10% or symptomatic hyperglycemia

Medicaid: Requires metformin trial unless contraindicated
May require sulfonylurea or SGLT2 inhibitor step prior to GLP-1 approval
MedicarePartD: Step therapy with metformin typically required
Quantity limits per 28-day supply common
CommercialPlans: CV indication may require documentation of established ASCVD
May require prior failure of two oral agents

ReauthorizationRequirements: Reduction in A1C by ≥0.5–1.0% after 3–6 months
Demonstrated adherence to therapy
Weight reduction of ≥3–5% from baseline if obesity targeted
No severe adverse events requiring discontinuation

2.Title: American Diabetes Association Standards of Care in Diabetes – 2024

OZEMPIC_V2.Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient has confirmed type 2 diabetes mellitus (ICD-10 E11.x) with persistent hyperglycemia despite optimized lifestyle modification and a ≥3-month trial of metformin at maximally tolerated dosing. Baseline A1C remains ≥7.0%, exceeding individualized glycemic targets. The patient also has established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, further increasing risk of major adverse cardiovascular events. Per the American Diabetes Association Standards of Care in Diabetes – 2024, GLP-1 receptor agonists such as semaglutide are recommended in patients with inadequate glycemic control and established ASCVD. Ozempic is FDA-approved both for glycemic improvement and cardiovascular risk reduction. Reauthorization will be based on objective improvement including ≥0.5–1.0% A1C reduction within 3–6 months and sustained adherence. Delaying escalation increases risk of microvascular and macrovascular complications. Ozempic is medically necessary and consistent with FDA-approved indications and current evidence-based guidelines.

Indications: Adjunct to diet and maximally tolerated statin therapy for adults with clinical atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) who require additional lowering of LDL-C
Adjunct to diet and other LDL-lowering therapies for adults with primary hyperlipidemia, including heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (HeFH), to reduce LDL-C
Homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (HoFH) as adjunct to other LDL-lowering therapies
PACriteria: ICD-10: E78.0 (pure hypercholesterolemia), E78.01 (familial hypercholesterolemia), I25.x (ASCVD)
Trial of high-intensity statin (e.g., atorvastatin 40–80 mg or rosuvastatin 20–40 mg) for ≥ 3 months unless contraindicated
Trial of ezetimibe for ≥ 3 months unless contraindicated
Persistent LDL-C ≥ 70 mg/dL despite ≥ 3 months of maximally tolerated statin ± ezetimibe
For HoFH: documented genetic or clinical diagnosis and inadequate LDL-C response to statin + ezetimibe + other LDL-lowering therapies
No history of serious hypersensitivity to evolocumab
Liver function and creatine kinase assessed prior to initiation

ASCVD_or_HeFH: Initiate high-intensity statin therapy (atorvastatin 40–80 mg or rosuvastatin 20–40 mg) for ≥ 3 months
Add ezetimibe if LDL-C remains ≥ 70 mg/dL after ≥ 3 months of statin
If LDL-C remains ≥ 70 mg/dL despite maximally tolerated statin ± ezetimibe for ≥ 3 months, escalate to PCSK9 inhibitor (evolocumab)
HoFH: Initiate statin and ezetimibe plus other LDL-lowering therapies (e.g., LDL apheresis) as appropriate
If LDL-C remains above individualized target after ≥ 3 months, add evolocumab

Medicaid: Requires documentation of statin and ezetimibe trial with dates and doses
LDL-C values before and after therapy required
MedicarePartD: Covered for ASCVD, HeFH, HoFH with documentation of step therapy and LDL-C thresholds
Prior authorization required
CommercialPlans: Prior authorization common; must document statin and ezetimibe intolerance or failure
LDL-C ≥ 70 mg/dL documentation required

ReauthorizationRequirements: ≥ 50% reduction in LDL-C within 3–6 months of initiation
Continued adherence to statin and ezetimibe unless contraindicated
No serious adverse effects or hypersensitivity

2.Title: 2018 AHA/ACC Multisociety Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol

REPATHA_V2b.Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient meets criteria for evolocumab (Repatha) therapy under FDA-approved indications. For ASCVD or HeFH, the patient has persistent LDL-C ≥ 70 mg/dL despite ≥ 3 months of maximally tolerated high-intensity statin and ezetimibe therapy, consistent with the 2018 AHA/ACC cholesterol guidelines and FDA labeling. For HoFH, the patient has inadequate LDL-C response to statin, ezetimibe, and other LDL-lowering therapies. Delaying PCSK9 inhibitor initiation increases risk of recurrent cardiovascular events. Repatha is medically necessary and consistent with contemporary guidelines and payer policy.

Indications: Chronic weight management in adults with obesity (BMI ≥ 30 kg/m²) or overweight (BMI ≥ 27 kg/m²) with at least one weight-related comorbidity
Weight reduction in adolescents ≥ 12 years with obesity (BMI ≥ 95th percentile for age and sex)
PACriteria: Age ≥ 12 years
BMI ≥ 30 kg/m² (obesity) or BMI ≥ 27 kg/m² (overweight) with ≥ 1 weight-related comorbidity
Documented trial of ≥ 3 months of lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, behavior) with inadequate weight loss
Failure, intolerance, or contraindication to alternative anti-obesity medications
Absence of personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or multiple endocrine neoplasia syndrome type 2
Not pregnant or breastfeeding
Baseline weight and BMI documented

FirstLine: Comprehensive lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, behavior) for ≥ 3 months
Consideration of alternative anti-obesity medications if lifestyle alone insufficient
SecondLine: Initiate Wegovy if lifestyle and alternative agents fail or are contraindicated
Titration: 0.25 mg weekly for 4 weeks, then 0.5 mg for 4 weeks, then 1.0 mg for 4 weeks, then 1.7 mg for 4 weeks, then 2.4 mg maintenance

Medicaid: Prior authorization required
Documentation of BMI ≥ 30 kg/m² or ≥ 27 kg/m² with comorbidity
Failure of lifestyle modification and alternative agents
MedicarePartD: Prior authorization required
Similar BMI and lifestyle failure documentation
CommercialPlans: Prior authorization required
BMI documentation and lifestyle failure required

ReauthorizationRequirements: ≥ 5% weight loss from baseline after 12 weeks on 2.4 mg
If < 5% loss, discontinue or consider alternative therapy
Ongoing adherence and tolerability assessment

2.Title: American Diabetes Association Standards of Care in Diabetes – 2024

WEGOVY_V2.Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient meets criteria for chronic weight management with Wegovy (semaglutide), a GLP-1 receptor agonist approved for obesity and overweight with weight-related comorbidities. The patient has documented obesity (BMI ≥ 30 kg/m²) or overweight (BMI ≥ 27 kg/m²) with comorbidity, and has failed ≥ 3 months of comprehensive lifestyle modification with inadequate weight loss. Alternative anti-obesity medications are contraindicated or not tolerated. Wegovy is FDA-approved and recommended by ADA for weight management in appropriate patients. Reauthorization will be based on ≥ 5% weight loss at 12 weeks; without this, therapy should be discontinued. Delaying escalation increases risk of obesity-related complications.

Indications: Stroke prevention in nonvalvular atrial fibrillation (NVAF)
Treatment and secondary prevention of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism (PE)
Prophylaxis of DVT/PE after hip or knee replacement surgery
Reduction of major cardiovascular events in patients with coronary artery disease (CAD) or peripheral artery disease (PAD)
PACriteria: ICD-10: I48.x (NVAF), I82.x (DVT), I26.x (PE), Z51.81 (post-hip/knee replacement), I25.x (CAD), I74.x (PAD)
Baseline renal function (CrCl) documented within 3 months
No active pathological bleeding
Not for use in patients with CrCl < 15 mL/min
No hypersensitivity to rivaroxaban
Pregnancy/lactation contraindicated
Use with caution in hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh B/C)

StrokePrevention_NVAF: Select rivaroxaban 20 mg daily (15 mg if CrCl 15–50 mL/min) with evening meal
Ensure adherence and renal monitoring at 6–12 months
DVT_PE_Treatment: Initial: 15 mg twice daily for 21 days
Monitor for bleeding and adherence at 3–6 months
HipKneeProphylaxis: Initiate 6–10 hours post-op once hemostasis is established
10 mg once daily for 35 days (hip) or 12 days (knee)
Assess wound healing and bleeding at discharge and 2–4 weeks
CAD_PAD: Add rivaroxaban 2.5 mg twice daily to aspirin (75–100 mg)
Monitor for bleeding and adherence at 3–6 months

Medicaid: Requires diagnosis-specific documentation (AF, DVT/PE, post-op, CAD/PAD)
Prior authorization common for non-formulary use
MedicarePartD: Covered for FDA indications with documentation
May require confirmation of renal function and indication
CommercialPlans: Prior authorization common for non-formulary use
Often preferred for NVAF and VTE indications

ReauthorizationRequirements: For NVAF: continued adherence and no major bleeding at 6–12 months
For DVT/PE: recurrence or bleeding assessment at 3–6 months
For post-op prophylaxis: adherence and wound assessment at discharge and 2–4 weeks
For CAD/PAD: adherence and bleeding assessment at 3–6 months

2.Title: CHEST Antithrombotic Therapy for VTE Disease – 2021

3.Title: AHA/ACC/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure – 2022

XARELTO_V2.Section2_AppealReadyNarrative: This patient meets criteria for rivaroxaban therapy under FDA-approved indications. In nonvalvular atrial fibrillation, the patient has CHA2DS2-VASc ≥ 2 or additional risk factors warranting anticoagulation. Per AHA/ACC/HFSA and CHEST, DOACs such as rivaroxaban are first-line for stroke prevention in NVAF and for treatment of VTE. In DVT/PE, rivaroxaban provides a simple oral regimen with noninferior efficacy and similar or lower bleeding risk compared with warfarin. In post-hip/knee replacement, rivaroxaban is FDA-approved for VTE prophylaxis with documented efficacy. In CAD/PAD, rivaroxaban 2.5 mg twice daily with aspirin reduces major adverse cardiovascular events. Delaying initiation increases thromboembolic risk and may necessitate bridging or alternative anticoagulation. Rivaroxaban is medically necessary and consistent with FDA labeling and contemporary guidelines.

Indications: Chronic weight management in adults with obesity (BMI ≥ 30 kg/m²) or overweight (BMI ≥ 27 kg/m²) with ≥ 1 weight-related comorbidity
Weight reduction in adolescents ≥ 12 years with obesity (BMI ≥ 95th percentile for age and sex)
PACriteria: Age ≥ 12 years
BMI ≥ 30 kg/m² (obesity) or BMI ≥ 27 kg/m² (overweight) with ≥ 1 weight-related comorbidity
Documented trial of ≥ 3 months of lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, behavior) with inadequate weight loss
Failure, intolerance, or contraindication to alternative anti-obesity medications
Absence of personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2
Not pregnant or breastfeeding
Baseline weight and BMI documented

FirstLine: Comprehensive lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, behavior) for ≥ 3 months
Consideration of alternative anti-obesity medications if lifestyle alone insufficient
SecondLine: Initiate Zepbound if lifestyle and alternative agents fail or are contraindicated
Titration: 2.5 mg weekly for 4 weeks, then 5 mg for 4 weeks, then 7.5 mg for 4 weeks, then 10 mg for 4 weeks, then 12.5 mg for 4 weeks, then 15 mg maintenance

Medicaid: Prior authorization required
Documentation of BMI ≥ 30 kg/m² or ≥ 27 kg/m² with comorbidity
Failure of lifestyle modification and alternative agents
MedicarePartD: Prior authorization required
Similar BMI and lifestyle failure documentation
CommercialPlans: Prior authorization required
BMI documentation and lifestyle failure required

ReauthorizationRequirements: ≥ 5% weight loss from baseline after 12 weeks on 15 mg
If < 5% loss, discontinue or consider alternative therapy
Ongoing adherence and tolerability assessment
